coredata.dc:title: Quadruple analyte responsive platform: Point-of-care testing and multi-coding logic computation based on metal ions recognition and selective response
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Zhe
Qing, Min
Fan, Yu Zhu
Yan, Hang
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: While it is recognized that instrumentation techniques can provide precise and sensitive solutions to heavy metal ion monitoring, it remains challenging to transform laboratory testing into a convenient, on-site, and quantitative sensing platform for point-of-care testing (POCT) in a resource-constrained setting. To address these limitations, an affordable and user-friendly colorimetric POCT sensing system is proposed here for selectively monitoring four metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+) based on the sulfur quantum dots (S dots). Quadruple distinct visual signals (green, brown, precipitation, and bright yellow) are presented on the fabricated paper-based analytical devices (PADs) when mixing S dots and metal ions. The high-quality photographs of the PADs are captured by a scanner, while a smartphone App converts visual signals to HSV values. The quantitative analysis relies on the digital colorimetric reading, and the limits of detection are 0.59, 0.47, 0.82, and 0.53 μM for Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, respectively. This metal ions-responsive platform is engineered as a smart strategy for multiple logic operations (YES, NOT, AND, INHIBIT, and NOR) by integrating multi-responsive blocks into the S dots with encoded patterns, which improves the computing capability. Accordingly, this strategy demonstrates its potential for on-site environmental testing and sophisticated molecular computation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of the S dots. (A) FTIR spectra of S dots (a) and PEG (b). (B) Survey XPS spectrum of S dots. (C) High-resolution XPS of S 2p. (D) TEM image of S dots. Inset: The corresponding HRTEM image (top) and the particle size distribution (down). (E) UV–vis absorption spectrum of S dots. (F) Partial magnification of the UV–vis absorption spectrum.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra (left) and photographs (right) of S dots for detecting Fe3+ (30 μM), Co2+ (30 μM), Cd2+ (50 μM), and Pb2+ (75 μM) in the absence and presence of EDTA. (B) Corresponding selectivity of S dots with various metal ions in terms of four different readout signal modes under the same conditions, respectively (The concentration of other metal ions was 150 μM).

Scanner-based POCT platform for metal ions monitoring. (A) Photographs of the colorimetric assay on the PAD for metal ions detection (Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+) with a scanner. Fitting curve between V and metal ions concentration: (B) Fe3+, (C) Co2+, (D) Cd2+, (E) Pb2+.

Multi-coding molecular logic computing based on visual changes of S dots. Multi-input and multi-output operation of YES (A) and NOT (B) logical based on color changes of S dots. (C) Logic gates of AND by combining Input A (Cd2+) with Input B (Pb2+) as inputs and the color change with precipitation as readout signals. (D) Proposed AND and INHIBIT logical by using Input A (Fe3+) or Input A′ (Co2+) and Input B (Pb2+) as inputs and visual responses (pale-green and precipitation, Y) and precipitation (Y′) as the output. (E) Cd2+ and Fe3+ as ternary input integrative NOR logic gate.
Schematic illustration of colorimetric detection of metal ions (Fe3 +, Co2 +, Pb2 +, and Cd2 +) using a scanner in conjunction with a PAD.

head.ce:title: Quadruple analyte responsive platform: Point-of-care testing and multi-coding logic computation based on metal ions recognition and selective response

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: While it is recognized that instrumentation techniques can provide precise and sensitive solutions to heavy metal ion monitoring, it remains challenging to transform laboratory testing into a convenient, on-site, and quantitative sensing platform for point-of-care testing (POCT) in a resource-constrained setting. To address these limitations, an affordable and user-friendly colorimetric POCT sensing system is proposed here for selectively monitoring four metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+) based on the sulfur quantum dots (S dots). Quadruple distinct visual signals (green, brown, precipitation, and bright yellow) are presented on the fabricated paper-based analytical devices (PADs) when mixing S dots and metal ions. The high-quality photographs of the PADs are captured by a scanner, while a smartphone App converts visual signals to HSV values. The quantitative analysis relies on the digital colorimetric reading, and the limits of detection are 0.59, 0.47, 0.82, and 0.53 μM for Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, respectively. This metal ions-responsive platform is engineered as a smart strategy for multiple logic operations (YES, NOT, AND, INHIBIT, and NOR) by integrating multi-responsive blocks into the S dots with encoded patterns, which improves the computing capability. Accordingly, this strategy demonstrates its potential for on-site environmental testing and sophisticated molecular computation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: For numerous biological activities, metal ions are necessary, as well as their regulation is required for the maintenance of proper functioning. However, once metal ions are present in high concentrations or coexist with other toxic metal ions in the environment, the beneficial properties are often counterbalanced by their poisonous effects, and even pose a danger to both the environment and human health. To control their proliferation in water and soil resources and protect human health, methods that can rapidly and accurately monitor the metal ions contamination in the ecological environment are needed. Toward this goal, several attempts such as inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Shih et al., 2016), atomic emission spectrometry (AES) (Kitazume et al., 1999), atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) (Ezoddin et al., 2010), and stripping voltammetry (SV) (Zhu et al., 2014) have been developed in state-of-the-art laboratories. Meanwhile, some nanomaterials with quantum-size effects, such as noble-metal quantum dots (Nemati and Zare-Dorabei, 2019), carbon nanoparticles (Song et al., 2018), and graphene quantum dots (Xia et al., 2020), make the fluorometric technique play a pivotal role in metal ion detection. Despite the advances in sensitivity and accuracy of such instrumentation techniques, they are restricted by the requirements for expensive instruments, complicated sample preparation, and trained laboratory professionals. As a result, developing an on-site quantitative testing platform that is affordable and portable is highly desirable.

In the last decade, the emerging point-of-care testing (POCT) platforms are more inclined to on-site, visual, and real-time detection rather than the aiding of analytical equipment, making it indispensable in food safety inspection (Hu et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2019), environmental monitoring (Sivakumar and Lee, 2021), and clinical diagnosis (Bhattacharjee et al., 2017; Xiong et al., 2020). Particularly, paper-based analytical devices (PADs) are an optimal choice for the POCT platform because of the following features of paper: (1) Paper is cheap, easy to handle and store (Qi et al., 2018); (2) The cellulose or cellulose-based polymer hydrophilic channels facilitate solution infiltration into the porous cellulose fibers (Wang et al., 2022). With the aid of a smartphone that possesses the bionic electronic eye-driven signal reader, it quantifies targets by converting the visually discernible color changes into color intensities. For example, Hou and co-workers described the color change generated by the reaction between nitrite and Griess reagent with RGB values, realizing the quantitative detection of nitrite in food samples by a colorimetric PAD (Hou et al., 2020). It is precisely indicated that PAD is a promising candidate for such a POCT platform with the advantages of small size, simplicity of operation, little reagent consumption, and good flexibility. However, another major issue is the light condition, since it affects the photography process and quality, resulting in reading mistakes and incorrect results. Efforts such as rescaling the RGB values (Jalal et al., 2017) and adding optical attachments (Kong et al., 2019) address this issue to a certain extent, but the practical applications of these output devices are still severely constrained by the differences among various smartphone models. In contrast, the flatbed scanner based on a charge-coupled-device (CCD) exhibits ideal imaging and repeatability functions because of the confined scanning space and steady light source (Hou et al., 2021; Medeiros and Lima, 2014). It is worth mentioning that Hou et al. employed a portable scanner with Wi-Fi transmission capability to obtain high-quality images of the PADs. Then they transmitted these pictures to the smartphone through wireless communication to obtain calibration curves of target concentrations (uric acid, glucose, and triglyceride) and RGB values, enabling biomarkers of cardiovascular diseases to be detected (Hou et al., 2021). Accordingly, taking advantage of a user-friendly interface and built-in high-performance sensing module, smartphones can not only simplify quantitative POCT system design, but also enable easy-to-use detection and are easily accessible to the public. Meanwhile, the usage of a scanner guarantees accuracy and sensitivity. Therefore, the POCT platform can break through the limitations of expensive instruments and high-trained laboratory professionals to enable on-site and real-time monitoring.

In portable optical sensing, such sensors commonly used noble metals such as Pt, Pd, Ag, and Au (Ju and Kim, 2015; Sun et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018), which undoubtedly increases the cost of POCT. Hence, noble-metals-free nanomaterials are considered to be the next generation of alternative sensors. As one of the Earth-abundant elements, sulfur finds a prominent place in various applications with diverse morphology, including nano, micro, and bulk states (Boyd, 2016; Teng et al., 2019). Among the reported applications, the nano-sized sulfur quantum dots (S dots) procure an edge on chemical sensing (Arshad and Sk, 2020; Liu et al., 2020), white light-emitting diodes (Wang et al., 2019), and cellular imaging (Qiao et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019) for their attractive optical property, desirable photostability, low toxicity, and superior dispersibility. However, there has been no attempt to design the S dots as a portable optical sensor for POCT. With the assistance of PEG-400 and NaOH, Zhang’s group successfully prepared the S dots with good water solubility from sublimated sulfur powder (Shen et al., 2018). At the same time, the disposable PAD is usually hydrophilic due to the abundance of –OH groups from cellulose (Kurdekar et al., 2016). Therefore, the S dots will easily infiltrate the filter paper, greatly reducing the time required for S dots to cover the detection area. From an economical cost and time saving point of view, S dots are a good choice for the PAD-based POCT platform.

Herein, we proposed a disposable PAD based on the S dots for POCT of four metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+) detection. Considering that these four metal ions combined with S dots produce different visual signals (Fe3+: from colorless to green; Co2+: from colorless to brown; Cd2+: from colorless to bright yellow; Pb2+: from colorless to precipitation), in addition to the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption, the corresponding color evolution can be used as a readout signal (HSV color model) to monitor the metal ions. As shown in Scheme 1, we designed and fabricated a three-layer PAD with several assay spots that a scanner can easily capture. The obtained images can be subsequently analyzed through the image processing software (ColorColl App) on a smartphone for visual and on-site sensing of metal ions. Moreover, the logic gate is a functional data processing device that allows logical calculations to perform when various signals are input (Zou et al., 2018). Therefore, it is possible to create molecular-scale computers and “autonomously regulated” chemical systems (Huang et al., 2011; Lee et al., 2021; Park et al., 2010). Through defining multi-input (Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+) and multi-readout signals (different colors and precipitation), we further constructed the multi-coding logic gates (YES, NOT, AND, INHIBIT, and NOR) based on the metal ions-induced S dots discrimination platform. This platform allows for flexible functional switching among the proposed logic gates via simply altering these input groups (metal ions) and defining different output signals. Compared with the existing logic gate with single input and output, this quadruple analyte responsive platform is beneficial for realizing large-scale molecular logic computation. Accordingly, the S dots-based platform shows remarkable versatility, enabling quadruple chemical sensing and advanced information processing by simply reading four independent visual signals. This strategy displays strong potential for S dots in developing on-site environmental monitoring and designing intelligent molecular engineering.

All chemicals were of at least analytical grade and used without further purification. Whatman grade 3 filter paper was purchased from Jinpan Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The other chemicals and apparatus required for this work, as well as the pretreatment of natural water samples, are detailed in the Supporting Information.

The S dots were synthesized according to the reported method with slight modifications (Shen et al., 2018). Typically, sublimed sulfur powder (1.4 g), 50 mL of water, PEG-400 (3 mL), and sodium hydroxide (4 g) were added to a 100 mL round-bottom flask. This round-bottom flask was connected to a glass condensed pipe and placed on a magnetic stirrer with a temperature controller. The mixture was stirred at 70 °C for 72 h. The prepared product was the sample of S dots. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting S dots were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min, and the collected supernatant was subsequently dialyzed (MWCO: 3500 Da) for 48 h to purify. Followed by freeze-drying, the solid samples were stored at room temperature and in dark conditions for further use.

The disposable paper-based analytical device (PAD) consisted of three layers of Whatman grade 3 filter papers, including the isolation, detection, and base layers. Firstly, the isolation and base layers were designed and fabricated. Then, circular test papers with a diameter of 8 mm were obtained by a common hole puncher. Subsequently, the test paper was submerged for about 30 min in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 5.0) containing S dots (40 μL/mL). After natural drying at room temperature, the detection layer was assembled with the other two layers using a multipurpose adhesive for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection procedure of scanner-based multiplex assay
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric reactions were performed in the disposable PAD. In brief, the circular assay spots with a diameter of 8 mm were immersed for 30 min in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 5.0) containing S dots. After natural drying at room temperature, they were glued between the isolation layer and the base. The standard solutions with varying metal ion concentrations were then added to the respective detecting zones on the PAD. Following that, the PADs were scanned with a desktop scanner (Laser MFP 136 W, Hewlett-Packard). Finally, the inspection images were captured and transferred to the smartphone, together with the data analyzed through a ColorColl App. Additionally, to verify the accuracy of the scanner-based detection platform, the metal ion samples were loaded to a 96-well microplate and analyzed by a multimode microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanner-based colorimetric assay in natural water
ce:section.ce:para: Referring to the reported approaches (Liu et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2019), the natural water was first treated to detect metal ions in actual samples. The detailed pretreatment process was described in the Supporting Information. According to the above-described strategy, the metal ions-spiked water samples were further measured on the PAD.

According to the previous reports (Shen et al., 2018), the S dots in a monodispersed state were prepared by a top-down method, where the sublimed sulfur powder was used as the sulfur source, polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG-400) as the protecting agent, and NaOH as the etchant. The successful formation of S dots is simple to prove. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy is used to confirm the surface groups of S dots and the capping of PEG-400 on S dots ( Fig. 1A). Compared with pure PEG-400, the characteristic bonds of PEG, originating from the stretching vibration of the –OH (3438 cm−1), the bending vibration of H–O–H (1658 cm−1), the stretching vibration of C–H (2879 cm−1), and the C–H bending vibration (1454 cm−1 and 1353 cm−1) (Mousavi et al., 2021; Nemati et al., 2018), are all observed in S dots. Meanwhile, the broad peak at 1120 cm−1 in PEG splits up into two sharper peaks at 1011 and 1164 cm−1 in the S dots, resulting from the stretching vibrations of the C–O–H (1164 cm−1) and the C–O–C (1011 cm−1) (Saboorizadeh et al., 2021; Shahbazi and Zare-Dorabei, 2019). These functional groups not only contribute to the well water solubility of the S dots, but also demonstrate that PEG is simply a passivating agent instead of involving the chemical reaction of S dots synthesis. Moreover, the S dots produce an obvious signal at 525 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of the S–S linkage (Arshad and Sk, 2020; Qiao et al., 2020). The elements and chemical composition of S dots are discussed by the X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) spectra. From Fig. 1B, the XPS spectrum of S dots mainly contains S, O, and C elements. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 1C), the S 2p band includes five peaks at 161.2, 162.4, 165.7, 167.3, and 168.5 eV, representing S2 - (2p 3/2), S2 - (2p 1/2), SO3 2-, SO4 2- (2p 3/2) and SO4 2- (2p 1/2), respectively. These results suggested that a large amount of sulfite and sulfate are attached to the surface of S dots, which is consistent with previous reports (Arshad and Sk, 2020). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows the formation of monodisperse quasispherical S dots with an average diameter of 3.4 nm and distinct lattice fringes (interplanar spacing = 0.22 nm) (Fig. 1D). As shown in Fig. 1E, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of S dots possesses three peaks at 230, 330, and 370 nm, respectively. The n → σ * transition of nonbonding electrons of S is responsible for the strong band at 230 nm (Shen et al., 2018), while the absorption peaks at 330 and 370 nm were ascribed to the existence of S2 2 - and S8 2 - on S dots surfaces (Wang et al., 2021). As expected, the ultra-small S dots with well water solubility were successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanner-based colorimetry on metal ions detection
ce:section.ce:para: It is critical to monitor heavy metal ions, including Cr3+/6+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Co2+, for environmental monitoring and food and water safety. Hence, the affinity of sulfur atoms toward metal ions motivated us to explore their potential for monitoring metal ions. Just as we expect, S dots can recognize Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+ through capturing four individual readout signals ( Fig. 2 A): They are the Fe3+-caused color change of colorless to green (increment of Abs625 nm), Co2+-caused color change of colorless to brown (increment of Abs330 nm. The UV–vis absorption was significantly enhanced at 330 nm after mixing S dots and Co2+, so the data analysis was performed by recording the peak at 330 nm in the subsequent experiments.), Pb2+-caused precipitation, and Cd2+-caused color change from colorless to bright yellow (increment of Abs445 nm). It is well known that ethylenediamine tetra-acetic acid (EDTA) is an effective masking agent for metal ions because it can form stable metal ion-EDTA complexes with metal ions. Therefore, EDTA was used to verify the feasibility of S dots-based colorimetric metal ions monitoring. From Fig. 2 A, in the presence of Fe3+, new peaks occur at 428, 526, and 625 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of S dots, and the corresponding color change (from colorless to green) is shown in the inset. Once EDTA was reacted with Fe3+ and then added to the S dots solution, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of S dots does not show new absorption peaks at 428, 526, and 625 nm nor a significant color change (inset). In the control experiment, the UV–vis absorption spectrum was not affected by EDTA when it was introduced into the S dots solution. This result indicated that the S dots-Fe3+ complexes were disintegrated due to the strong binding between EDTA and Fe3+. Similarly, S dots show prominent signal responses towards Co2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+ without the addition of EDTA. While these metal ions were first exposed to EDTA and then mixed with S dots, there were neither new absorption peaks nor visual changes (Fig. 2 A). These findings suggested that the EDTA-metal ions congeries can prevent metal ions from binding to S dots. Therefore, the successful substitution of EDTA in S dots further demonstrates that the colorimetric detection originates from the coordination binding of S dots-metal ions.

Since real water samples contain various interfering species (metallic cations and common anions) that may interfere with the detection of these four metal ions and give false-positive signals, the selectivity of the proposed method was evaluated for different interfering substances and tested at higher concentrations (i.e. 150 μM, namely 2 – 5-fold higher than the linear maximum concentration of these four metal ions). In addition, the choice of interfering species concentrations for selective detection of metal ions was similar to previous reports (Nemati and Zare-Dorabei, 2019; Wang et al., 2022). In contrast with other metal ions (such as Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Ag+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Au3+, K+, and Na+), these four readout signal modes exhibit high selectivity for the determination of Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+ (Fig. 2B). Moreover, we examined possible responses to eleven different types of anions. These anions have no effect on S dots at large concentrations (Fig. S1). The corresponding photographs of S dots with different metal ions and common anions are given in Fig. S2. Employing S dots as a colorimetric probe, four metal ions can be distinguished by quadruple visual signals. In comparison with other single readout signal reports, this method is available to minimize possible interference.

Considering the noticeable color variation, S dots are beneficial for the portable on-site colorimetric monitoring of metal ions. Typically, the optimal experimental conditions are given in Fig. S3 (Concentration: S dots = 40 μL/mL; pH = 5.4, HAc-NaAc buffer). Meanwhile, the kinetic monitoring of the reaction between S dots and metal ions (taking Fe3+ as an example) was investigated. Fig. S4 illustrates that this colorimetric reaction can be completed in a relatively short time (30 min), allowing for on-site POCT assays of metal ions. The preparation procedure of a PAD, including the isolation layer, detection layer, and base, is given in Fig. S5. Initially, the size and shape of portable PADs were designed suitable for colorimetric assays with a scanner, maximizing the scanning capabilities of the device on rather large samples. Moreover, an assay region (3 rows × 11 columns) was designed in the detection layer for performing colorimetric analysis, in which each row was used to prepare individual metal ions, and the columns were used to repeat the experiments. Only the detection layer is hydrophilic, as they are easily and uniformly wetted by the testing solution. In contrast, the other two layers are hydrophobic, for confining the assay zones and preventing pollution of the assay layer and scanner.

Further, the scanner-based POCT performance of the S dots platform for metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+) detection was evaluated. As expected, the high-quality images are available to be captured with a scanner by reducing the illumination levels in the confined scanning space ( Fig. 3A). In contrast, the pictures obtained with a smartphone (iPhone 8 plus) are susceptible to light conditions, resulting in color deviations seen with the naked eye (Fig. S6). Additionally, the scanning process can be completed within 2 min, without sophisticated operations, such as modulating the focal length and aperture. Therefore, the scanner is ideal for performing the colorimetric assay in this study. As displayed in Fig. 3A, there is a significant correlation between the metal ions concentrations and the color intensity of the respective detection regions. For quantitative analysis with a PAD, in addition to the RGB color model, the HSV color mode is also capable of accomplishing this task. The HSV color model converts color information into hue (H), saturation (S), and value (V). This model simulates the way that colors are organized and conceptualized in human vision, giving it better linear scalability and greater sensitivity than other RGB spaces (Fan et al., 2019; Su et al., 2015). With the assistance of the ColorColl app, it is a natural choice to employ the HSV model for digital colorimetric assay. The screen captures of this analysis process are given in Fig. S7. As displayed in Fig. 3B, with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, the V channel value (a part of the HSV color model) gradually decreases, together with a good linear relationship in the range of 0.8 – 75 μM (R2 = 0.9923). According to the “3σ/k” rule (where k is the slope of the V channel value vs. metal ion concentration, and σ is the standard deviation of 11 blank signals), the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was calculated to be 0.59 μM. Meanwhile, we found that there are good linear relationships between the V channel value and the different concentrations of Co2+ (0.75 – 100 μM), Cd2+ (1– 60 μM), and Pb2+ (0.75 – 50 μM) (Fig. 3C-E). The corresponding LODs were estimated to be 0.47, 0.82, and 0.53 μM for Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+ (3σ/k), respectively. To ensure the reliability of the results, each sample was repeated five times. Since visible precipitation can be produced when Pb2+ is more than 60 μM, the V channel value will be affected. As a result, we only quantified Pb2+ at low concentrations. Compared with other metal ions detection protocols (Table S1), such as noble metal nanoparticles-based colorimetric sensing strategies (Liao et al., 2017; Mehta et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2012) and fluorescence analysis based on carbon nanodots (Du et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2015), this designed S dots-based platform displays superior sensitivity. It is speculated that the affinity of S dots for metal ions ensures the sensitivity for detecting metal ions.

In addition, to demonstrate the good performance of the HSV color model in the colorimetric assay, the quantitative results of the RGB color model are given in Fig. S8. Although Fe3+, Co2+, and Pb2+ in various concentrations have linear relationships with each channel (R, G, B) in RGB color mode, as well as total R + G + B intensity, the fittings of the standard curves are not satisfactory. In addition, Cd2+ cannot be quantified by RGB color mode. The detailed linear range and R2 are summarized in Table S2. In terms of linear range and R2, the RGB model had poorer sensitivity and correlation in the fitting standard curves in comparison with the HSV model. Therefore, the advantages of this digital colorimetric assay with the HSV model were manifested in the linearity of the fitting equation and the gradient change of detection value for each metal ion concentration.

The success of establishing the linear correlation between the V channel value of PADs and the corresponding metal ion concentrations motivated us to further validate the accuracy of this POCT technique by comparing the results with standard spectrophotometric measurement. Metal ion solutions with the same concentrations were prepared, and their UV–vis absorption spectra can be measured with a multimode microplate reader. Fig. S9 exhibits the correlation plot between the V channel value measured with the smartphone and the absorbance obtained by the multimode microplate reader. By comparing the two measurements, we found that they correlated well with each other (R2 > 0.98), demonstrating the robustness of the POCT technique together with the reliability of the color intensity to quantify metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the portable colorimetric platform
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction mechanism between S dots and filter paper was firstly investigated. The disposable paper-based analysis device (PAD) used in this paper was made of Whatman cellulose filter paper (Grade 3), which is hydrophilic due to the abundance of –OH groups (Kurdekar et al., 2016). In addition, S dots have good water solubility, so the filter paper is easily infiltrated by S dots solution. Whatman filter paper has a large surface area and high absorption rate, so S dots can be adsorbed into the internal fibrous multi-slit structure through the process of physical adsorption, greatly reducing the time required for S dots to cover the detection area (Hao et al., 2020; Qin et al., 2021). At the same time, the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups of the S dots can form hydrogen bonds with the –OH on the filter paper, resulting in strong binding between S dots and filter paper.

According to the reports, the soft acid-soft base interaction between metal ions and S atoms allows metal ions to adhere to S dots through strong coordination chemistry (Anh et al., 2017). The S dots can aggregate and form a CoxSy type complex with the addition of Co2+, resulting in the change of the optical signals. Moreover, due to lone pair electrons in the sulfur atom, Pb2+ induced changes in the S dots morphology and the optical signal (Arshad and Sk, 2020). In addition, DLS and TEM as well as XPS techniques were used to study the reaction mechanism between metal ions and S dots. As shown in Fig. S10A, the S dots are no longer uniformly dispersed but tend to aggregate to varying degrees with the addition of Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, respectively: Fe3+ induces S dots to form large irregular particles; S dots form large aggregates with an ambiguous boundary when exposed to Pb2+; upon addition of Cd2+, S dots produce continuous necklace-like nanoparticles; S dots are aggregated to form a compact structure when treated with Co2+. Correspondingly, the hydrated particle sizes of S dots also increase obviously (Fig. S10B). Therefore, the metal ion-induced aggregation indicated a strong coordination chemistry between sulfur and metal ions. The full scan XPS spectra were also conducted after the S dots were treated with the four metal ions. In Fig. S10C, curve (a) proves that S dots contain S, O, and C elements. Once S dots are mixed with Fe3+, the new binding energy observed around 710.2 eV is attributed to Fe 2p elements (curve b) (Ansari et al., 2020). Compared to the spectrum of S dots, the binding energy of the peaks for Co 2p is displayed at 780.6 eV (curve c) (Wang et al., 2020). The characteristic peaks are observed at approximately 404.0 and 411.3 eV in the presence of Cd2+, which are assigned to Cd 3d3/2 and Cd 3d5/2, respectively (Chen et al., 2015). With the addition of Pb2+, the characteristic peaks at approximately 414 and 138.3 eV correspond to Pb 4d and Pb 4 f (curve e), respectively (Lyu et al., 2021). As a result, this colorimetric sensing is attributed to the affinity of S dots for metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis by scanner-based portable system
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practicality of this platform, we employed the established method to monitor four metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+) in Jialing River water samples. Meanwhile, the results were verified with the UV–vis absorption spectra. As summarized in Table S3, no metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+) were found in Jialing River water. For the samples spiked with metal ions, it can be observed that the values tested by this suggested strategy are consistent with the results measured by the UV–vis absorption spectra and the spiked values for metal ions concentrations. The recoveries in the samples range from 92.48% to 108.40% for Fe3+, from 94.72% to 113.40% for Co2+, from 93.28% to 106.53% for Cd2+, from 107.14% to 117.6% for Pb2+. The relative standard deviations (RSD) obtained by five times repeated experiments are below 5.03%. The above results illustrated that the scanner-based POCT platform is applicable and reliable for metal ions detection in natural water samples.

Inspired by the above-described color variation events, multiple logic operations were designed by adjusting the inputs (Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+) into S dots and recording the visual readout signals (color changes and precipitation). In the YES logic operation, metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+) were employed as inputs, while the naked-eye signals of S dots served as outputs. The presence and absence of the metal ion are defined as “1” and “0”, respectively. For output, the colorless color of S dots was described as “0” and visual change differing from colorless (color changes or precipitation) as “1”. Therefore, the multi-input and multi-output YES logic was constructed with different input and output groups of green (Fe3+), brown (Co2+), bright yellow (Cd2+), and precipitation (Pb2+) ( Fig. 4 A). With the same operation, this YES logic can be modulated into NOT simply by changing the output as colorless color (Fig. 4B). In addition, the events of multiple color changes enable a series of complex parallel multi-coded logic computing systems. Above all, metal ions were incubated in arbitrary pair combinations, and their corresponding optical responses were recorded (Fig. S11). As long as Co2+ runs into S dots solution, the visual changes between S dots and Fe3+(colorless to brown), Cd2+ (colorless to brown), and Pb2+ (no precipitation) are largely inhibited (Fig. S11A-C). It illustrated that the reaction between S dots and Co2+ is much faster than Fe3+, Cd2+, and Pb2+. The bright yellow color produced by Cd2+-induced S dots is also blocked by Fe3+ (Fig. S11D). From Fig. S11E and F, neither Fe3+ nor Cd2+ runs into S dots solution can influence the deposition of Pb2+, and at the same time, visual signals of color change and precipitation are produced when Pb2+ is mixed with Fe3+ (colorless to pale green and precipitation) and Cd2+ (colorless to pale yellow and precipitation), respectively. Hence, two AND logic gates are able to be constructed by selecting Fe3+/Pb2+ or Cd2+/Pb2+ as Input A/Input B and defining the visual changes (colorless to pale yellow/green and precipitation) as the output signal (Fig. 4 C and D). The AND logic gives the signals of pale yellow and precipitation (output = 1) in the presence of Cd2+ and Pb2+ (1/1). Similarly, the addition of Fe3+ and Pb2+ (1/1) triggers pale green and precipitation (readout = 1). On the contrary, with neither no input (0/0) nor with one input of Fe3+ or Pb2+ (1/0 or 0/1; Cd2+ or Pb2+, 1/0 or 0/1) would the logical AND operation to exhibit the signal (output = 0). Once we define Co2+ as Input A′ and precipitation as the output, the AND logic based on metal ions-induced S dots platform can invert to the binary INHIBIT logic gate (Fig. 4D). Only the absence of Co2+ (Input A′) and the presence of Pb2+ (Input B) (0/1) lead to precipitation (output = 1). For NOR logic performing, the initial state of colorless served as readout signal (output = 1), and this original state is maintained only when there are no metal ions as input. Otherwise, all the input groups (1/0, 0/1, and 1/1) are unable to trigger the NOR logic and give the output = 0 (Fig. 4E). Compared to the routine molecular logic with limited inputs and a single output, the multi-input and multi-output platform based on the metal ions-induced S dots are beneficial for the concept of multi-coding and enhancement of the computing capability.

Benefiting from the affinity between sulfur atom and metal ions, we surprisingly found that S dots can recognize Fe3+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+ by capturing four individual visual signals (Fe3+: from colorless to green; Co2+: from colorless to brown; Cd2+: from colorless to bright yellow; Pb2+: from colorless to precipitation). To alleviate the difficulty of on-site metal ion monitoring in a resource-constrained setting, a portable and easy-to-use colorimetric POCT sensing platform was presented to discriminate four metal ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+) based on S dots. The quadruple metal ions visual platform is performed on a portable PAD with several assay spots where a scanner can easily capture high-quality images. With the aid of ColorColl, a smartphone App, the distinct visual changes (green, brown, bright yellow, and precipitation) can be converted into HSV values to achieve quantitative detection of metal ions. Compared with conventional spectrophotometric measurements, the scanner-based system showed good agreement in detecting four metal ions, suggesting its potential for reliable POCT of environmental testing in low-resource areas. Therefore, this S dots-based strategy showed good performance and advantages, such as low cost, good selectivity, high accuracy and portability, together with user-friendliness. The durability and recyclability of PAD needs further improvement. Additionally, multi-coding logic gates were constructed by integrating different metal ions and the quadruple visual signals, enhancing the computing capability by comparing with the routine logic gate with limited inputs and a single output. We expected the S dots-based sensor to further realize advanced intelligent sensing and molecular information technologies in complex environmental samples.

Heavy metal poisoning usually corresponds to the accumulation of heavy metals in the human body, even causing severe damage to the developing brain. Hence, accurate and sensitive metal ion detection methods are widely sought. However, laboratory testing is restricted by the requirements for expensive instruments and high-trained personnel in low-resource settings. Herein, a scanner-assisted colorimetric point-of-care testing platform is proposed for selective monitoring of four metal ions in natural water samples. This portable sensing system overcomes the cost and time limitations of laboratory methods and provides a smart strategy for sophisticated molecular computation by constructing multiple logic operations.
